section.title: The Prince of Tyre and Against Sidon
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the Tyre sequence with two oracles, one against the ruler of Tyre for claiming to be a god, and a second lament for the king of Tyre cast in imagery of Eden and a guardian cherub, before turning briefly to judge neighboring Sidon and closing with a promise of Israel's secure return to her own land.
section.thesis: Human pride that claims divine status, however wise or wealthy it becomes, will be humbled to the dust by the God who alone is truly exalted, while His own scattered people will one day be gathered home to dwell securely, free from every hostile neighbor.
section.frameNote: Four movements: judgment on the ruler of Tyre for claiming to be a god; a lament over the king of Tyre using imagery of Eden and a guardian cherub cast down; a shorter oracle against Sidon; and the promise that Israel will dwell securely in her own land.

units.label: The ruler of Tyre judged
units.note: I am a god, you are but a man

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, tell the ruler of Tyre that this is what the Lord GOD says: Your heart is proud, and you have said, 'I am a god; I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the sea.' Yet you are a man and not a god, though you have regarded your heart as that of a god.
Behold, you are wiser than Daniel; no secret is hidden from you!
By your wisdom and understanding you have gained your wealth and amassed gold and silver for your treasuries.
By your great skill in trading you have increased your wealth, but your heart has grown proud because of it.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Because you regard your heart as the heart of a god,
behold, I will bring foreigners against you, the most ruthless of nations. They will draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom and will defile your splendor.
They will bring you down to the {{bor|Pit}}, and you will die a violent death in the heart of the seas.
Will you still say, 'I am a god,' in the presence of those who slay you? You will be only a man, not a god, in the hands of those who wound you.
You will die the death of the uncircumcised at the hands of foreigners. For I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD.”

units.label: A lament for the king of Tyre
units.note: in Eden, a guardian cherub, cast down

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, take up a lament for the king of Tyre and tell him that this is what the Lord GOD says: 'You were the seal of {{tokniyt|perfection}}, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty.

You were in Eden, the garden of God. Every kind of precious stone adorned you: ruby, topaz, and diamond, beryl, onyx, and jasper, sapphire, turquoise, and emerald. Your mountings and settings were crafted in gold, prepared on the day of your creation.
You were anointed as a guardian {{keruv|cherub}}, for I had ordained you. You were on the holy mountain of God; you walked among the fiery stones.
From the day you were created you were blameless in your ways, until wickedness was found in you.
By the vastness of your trade, you were filled with violence, and you sinned. So I drove you in disgrace from the mountain of God, and I banished you, O guardian cherub, from among the fiery stones.
Your heart grew proud of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom because of your splendor; so I cast you to the earth; I made you a spectacle before kings.

By the multitude of your iniquities and the dishonesty of your trading you have profaned your sanctuaries. So I made fire come from within you, and it consumed you. I reduced you to ashes on the ground in the eyes of all who saw you.
All the nations who know you are appalled over you. You have come to a horrible end and will be no more.'”
Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against her.

And you are to declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: 'Behold, I am against you, O Sidon, and I will be glorified within you. They will know that I am the LORD when I execute judgments against her and demonstrate My holiness through her.
I will send a plague against her and shed blood in her streets; the slain will fall within her, while the sword is against her on every side. Then they will know that I am the LORD.
For the people of Israel will no longer face a pricking brier or a painful thorn from all around them who treat them with contempt. Then they will know that I am the Lord GOD.'

units.label: Israel gathered in security
units.note: the LORD shown holy among them

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: 'When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they have been scattered, I will show Myself holy among them in the sight of the nations. Then they will dwell in their own land, which I have given to My servant Jacob.

And there they will dwell securely, build houses, and plant vineyards. They will dwell securely when I execute judgments against all those around them who treat them with contempt. Then they will know that I am the LORD their God.'”

bor.gloss: The Pit, a term for the grave or the realm of the dead. The proud ruler who claimed to sit 'in the seat of gods' is brought down to this lowest of destinations, a deliberate irony between his claimed height and his actual end.

tokniyt.gloss: A pattern, measure, or perfection, here rendered 'seal of perfection.' The term describes something measured out completely, whole and lacking nothing, a description that, applied to a creature rather than the Creator, becomes the very seedbed of pride.

keruv.gloss: A cherub, an exalted angelic being elsewhere pictured guarding Eden's entrance and the ark of the covenant. Whether this title describes the human king of Tyre in extravagant royal metaphor or points through him to a heavenly being fallen through pride is genuinely debated, and this passage is read carefully with that honest uncertainty.

basic.context: This chapter completes the three chapter sequence on Tyre with two closely related oracles: a plain judgment on Tyre's ruler for claiming divine status because of his wisdom and wealth, and then a lament for 'the king of Tyre' cast in extraordinary imagery, Eden, precious stones, and a guardian cherub, before turning briefly to a shorter oracle against neighboring Sidon and closing with a hopeful promise that Israel will one day dwell securely in her own land.
basic.hermeneutics: The first oracle, against the ruler or prince of Tyre, verses 1 to 10, clearly describes a proud human king judged for claiming to be a god. The second passage, the lament for the king of Tyre, verses 11 to 19, uses language so exalted, Eden, the garden of God, a guardian cherub, the holy mountain, that faithful readers have long debated whether it describes the human king alone in poetic hyperbole or uses him as a lens onto a greater fallen being behind human pride. This passage holds both readings with appropriate humility rather than forcing a single conclusion.
basic.originalLanguages: The king is called a keruv, a cherub, an exalted term used elsewhere for the beings who guard Eden and the ark, applied here either as royal metaphor or as a genuine glimpse of a heavenly power behind human pride. He is called the seal of tokniyth, perfection or completeness, a description properly belonging to God alone. And his end is the bor, the Pit, the lowest possible destination for one who claimed the highest possible seat.
basic.typology: The garden of Eden, the holy mountain, and the guardian cherub cast down for pride form a rich typological backdrop echoing Genesis 3's fall and anticipating the final judgment of every proud power, whether human or spiritual, before the God who alone remains exalted forever.
basic.christ: Where the king of Tyre corrupted his wisdom because of his splendor and was cast down from the mountain of God, Christ, though truly divine, did not grasp at equality with God but humbled Himself, and was for that humility exalted to the highest place, Philippians 2:5-11, the perfect reversal of Tyre's proud self exaltation and fall.
basic.god: God alone is truly exalted; every claim to divine status made by a creature, however wise, wealthy, or beautiful, will be shown false and humbled to dust. Yet the chapter does not end in mere judgment; it closes with God's tender promise to gather His scattered people home to dwell securely, showing that His holiness is displayed not only in judging pride but in keeping covenant love.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3:1-24 gives the pattern of pride, a garden, and a serpent figure cast down that this lament clearly echoes. Isaiah 14:12-15 gives a strikingly similar lament over the king of Babylon, long read alongside this passage as together hinting at a fallen heavenly power behind proud human rulers. Philippians 2:5-11 contrasts Tyre's self exaltation with Christ's humility and consequent exaltation. Revelation 21:1-4 answers this chapter's Eden imagery with the final restoration of God dwelling securely with His people.
basic.oneStory: Human pride that claims to sit in God's own seat is always, finally, humbled to the Pit, however wise or beautiful it appears; yet God's greater purpose is not mere judgment but gathering His scattered people home to dwell securely with Him, a promise fulfilled and secured forever in Christ, who alone was truly exalted because He first humbled Himself.

context.literary: The climax and close of the three chapter Tyre sequence, 26 to 28, moving from a direct oracle against Tyre's living ruler to an extraordinary lament using cosmic imagery for 'the king of Tyre,' before a brief coda against Sidon and a closing promise of Israel's secure restoration that also closes the whole section on Israel's near neighbors before the Egypt oracles begin in chapter 29.
context.historical: Tyre in this period was ruled by a succession of kings whose wealth from maritime trade was legendary in the ancient world; the 'ruler' and 'king' addressed in this chapter's two oracles are best understood as the same office, likely referring to Ithobaal II or a similar contemporary ruler, though the lament's imagery reaches beyond any one historical figure to something larger.
context.canonical: Closes the Tyre sequence and stands alongside Isaiah 14's lament over the king of Babylon as the two Old Testament passages most often discussed together regarding pride, a fall from a high position, and the possible presence of a spiritual power behind a human throne.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose and humble the pride of Tyre's ruler, who claimed divine status because of his wisdom and wealth, using imagery so exalted for the second lament that it forces the reader to see human pride itself as an echo of the very first fall in Eden.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes the judgment of a specific ancient ruler's specific sin, self deifying pride; it does not prescribe how to address every proud leader today. Its abiding principle is that pride which claims for itself what belongs to God alone will be humbled, whoever holds it.

debates.question: Does the lament over the king of Tyre, verses 11 to 19, describe only a proud human king in extravagant poetic language, or does it also picture the fall of Satan behind that human pride?

views.label: A hyperbolic lament describing a proud human king alone
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern royal ideology commonly described kings in exalted, even semi divine language, comparing them to Eden or the gods; read this way, the whole lament, garden, cherub, fiery stones, is extravagant court poetry applied to Tyre's actual, historical ruler, whose trade made him wealthy and whose wealth made him proud, exactly as the preceding oracle in verses 1 to 10 plainly states about the same figure.

views.label: The human king's fall pictures, or is overlaid with, the fall of a heavenly being, traditionally identified with Satan
views.case: Details that stretch beyond any human king, being anointed as a cherub, walking among the fiery stones, being blameless from creation until wickedness was found, closely parallel language many readers connect to Isaiah 14's similarly exalted lament over the king of Babylon and to later Jewish and Christian traditions about an angelic fall through pride; on this reading the human king becomes a kind of window onto the greater spiritual pride and fall that stands behind and inspires human tyranny.

debates.bearing: Because faithful, careful readers genuinely disagree here, and because Scripture itself does not settle the question explicitly in this text, both views are presented with humility; either way, the passage's clear moral center holds, that pride which claims what belongs to God alone will be brought down, whether the immediate reference is a human king, a fallen spiritual power, or both together.

study.originalLanguages: Tyre's king is addressed as a keruv, a cherub, exalted language elsewhere used for the beings who guard Eden and the ark, whether as extravagant royal metaphor or a genuine glimpse of a fallen spiritual power behind human pride. He is called the seal of tokniyth, perfection, a completeness that belongs to God alone. And his end is the bor, the Pit, the lowest destination for one who claimed the highest seat.

typology.type: A garden, a holy mountain, and a guardian cherub cast down for pride
typology.fulfillment: Echoes Genesis 3's first fall and anticipates the final judgment of every proud power before the God who alone remains exalted, a pattern fully answered in Christ's humility and exaltation, Philippians 2:5-11.

study.christ: Where the king of Tyre corrupted his wisdom for the sake of his splendor and was cast down from the mountain of God, Christ, being truly divine, did not grasp at equality with God but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, and was for that humility exalted to the highest place, given the name above every name, Philippians 2:5-11, the perfect reversal of Tyre's proud self exaltation and consequent fall to the Pit.
study.god: God alone is truly exalted; every creature's claim to divine status, however wise, wealthy, or beautiful, will be exposed as false and humbled to dust. Yet judgment on pride is not God's last word in this chapter; it closes with His tender promise to gather His scattered people home to dwell securely in their own land, showing that His holiness is displayed as fully in covenant faithfulness as in judgment on arrogance.

crossReferences.note: The pattern of pride, a garden setting, and a serpent figure cast down that this lament for the king of Tyre clearly echoes.
A strikingly similar lament over the king of Babylon, long read alongside this passage in discussions of pride and a possible fallen heavenly power behind human rulers.
Christ's humility and consequent exaltation stand in perfect contrast to Tyre's self exaltation and fall.
Answers this chapter's Eden imagery and Israel's promised secure dwelling with the final restoration of God dwelling with His people forever.

study.oneStory: Human pride that claims to sit in God's own seat is always, finally, humbled to the Pit, however wise, wealthy, or beautiful it appears. Yet God's larger purpose in this chapter is not mere judgment but gathering His scattered people home to dwell securely with Him, a promise secured forever in Christ, who alone was truly exalted because He first humbled Himself to the point of death.

quiz.q: What specific claim did the ruler of Tyre make that provoked judgment in verses 1 to 10?

options.text: He said, 'I am a god; I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the sea.'
options.note: Verse 2 quotes this exact claim as the provoking sin.

options.text: He refused to pay taxes to Babylon.
options.note: Not mentioned in this passage; the stated sin is his claim to divine status.

options.text: He built an idol temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: Not described here; the accusation concerns his own self deification, not idol building elsewhere.

options.text: He was simply wealthier than any other ancient king.
options.note: Wealth itself is mentioned, verses 4 to 5, but the specific provoking sin is his claim to be a god, not wealth alone.

quiz.q: What imagery does the lament over the king of Tyre, verses 11 to 19, use to describe him?

options.text: He was in Eden, the garden of God, anointed as a guardian cherub, walking among the fiery stones.
options.note: Verses 13 to 14 use exactly this exalted imagery.

options.text: He is compared to a common shepherd tending sheep.
options.note: The imagery used is Eden and a guardian cherub, not shepherding language.

options.text: He is compared to a soldier defending a border fortress.
options.note: The passage's imagery is cosmic and Edenic, not military fortress language.

options.text: He is compared to a merchant selling goods in a marketplace.
options.note: Trade is mentioned as the source of his pride, verse 16, but the central imagery of the lament itself is Eden and cherub language.

quiz.q: How does this passage handle the debate over whether the lament describes only a human king or also pictures a fallen spiritual power?

options.text: It presents both readings honestly, since faithful interpreters genuinely disagree and Scripture does not explicitly settle the question in this text.
options.note: This reflects a careful, humble approach consistent with how the debate is framed for this passage.

options.text: It insists dogmatically that the passage is only ever about Satan and never about a human king.
options.note: This overstates certainty the text itself does not provide; the preceding oracle, verses 1 to 10, plainly addresses a human ruler.

options.text: It insists dogmatically that the passage could never refer to anything beyond an ordinary human king.
options.note: This dismisses a long, serious interpretive tradition too quickly; the imagery genuinely exceeds ordinary royal description.

options.text: It claims the debate has no bearing on the passage's moral meaning at all.
options.note: The debate does not change the clear moral center, that pride which claims what belongs to God is humbled, but it is not irrelevant to how the passage is read.

quiz.q: What is Sidon judged for in verses 20 to 24?

options.text: The LORD announces He will be glorified and shown holy by executing judgments against her, ending her role as a painful thorn to Israel.
options.note: Verses 22 to 24 tie Sidon's judgment to God's glory and to removing a lasting source of contempt against Israel.

options.text: Sidon is judged for a specific act of gloating over Jerusalem's fall, exactly like Tyre.
options.note: Unlike the earlier Tyre and Ammon oracles, no specific gloating quote from Sidon is recorded here; the emphasis is on God's glory being shown through judgment.

options.text: Sidon is condemned for claiming to be a god, exactly like the ruler of Tyre.
options.note: That specific claim belongs to the ruler of Tyre, verse 2, not to Sidon in this shorter oracle.

options.text: Sidon is condemned solely for economic rivalry with Tyre.
options.note: The text does not describe rivalry with Tyre as the reason; it centers on God's glory and holiness being displayed through judgment.

quiz.q: How does the chapter conclude, in verses 25 to 26?

options.text: With a promise that Israel will be gathered from the nations to dwell securely in her own land, building houses and planting vineyards.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 explicitly promise this secure gathering and settled life for Israel.

options.text: With a warning that Israel will face renewed judgment before Sidon does.
options.note: The passage's tone here shifts to hope and promise for Israel, not further warning against her.

options.text: With a final oracle against Egypt introduced immediately.
options.note: The Egypt oracles begin in the next chapter, 29, not within this chapter's closing verses.

options.text: With an unresolved question about Israel's future left open.
options.note: The passage closes with a clear, positive promise, not an unresolved or open ended question.

questions: The ruler of Tyre let his wisdom and wealth convince him he was like a god. What success or ability in your own life tempts you to feel unaccountable to God?

The king of Tyre is described as blameless 'until wickedness was found' in him, corrupted by his own beauty and trade. Where has something genuinely good in your life quietly become a source of pride rather than gratitude?

This passage holds two views on the king of Tyre's lament with honesty rather than forcing one answer. Where else in your walk with God do you need to hold a hard question with humility rather than pretending to certainty you don't have?

Christ, unlike the king of Tyre, did not grasp at equality with God but humbled Himself, and was exalted as a result. Where is God inviting you to choose humility this week rather than self exaltation?

The chapter ends with Israel gathered to dwell securely, no longer surrounded by hostile neighbors. What would it look like for you to live today out of the security of that promised future rather than out of anxiety about present threats?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew distinguishes 'ruler' (nagid) in the first oracle, verses 1 to 10, from 'king' (melek) in the lament, verses 11 to 19; most interpreters take both titles as referring to the same office or figure, though the shift in title has fed the debate over whether the lament reaches beyond the human ruler alone.
The precise identification of some of the nine listed gemstones is uncertain in Hebrew and varies between translations; the general sense of complete, priceless adornment is not affected.

The phrase 'guardian cherub' translates language whose exact grammatical force is debated among Hebrew scholars, some reading it as 'with an anointed guardian cherub I placed you' rather than identifying the king himself as the cherub; this uncertainty is part of why the passage is read with interpretive humility.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Ezekiel 28:1-26. No verified sermon from the trusted cluster directly expounding this specific passage was found this session; sermon status is none. The debate on Ezekiel 28:11-19 is presented with interpretive humility per instructions, offering both the hyperbolic human king reading and the traditional fallen cherub reading without asserting one as certain.
